Governance found these problems:

- API and workflow persist different Quality evidence.
- Quality timing has conflicting sources of truth.
- Existing target-app Quality commands lack migration, potentially becoming silent no-ops.
- Provider/parsing failures can leave operations running and uncancellable.
- Manual and workflow Quality checks can race on the same candidate.
- Agent-reported commands aren’t tied to observed supervised executions.
- Candidate commit and worktree integrity aren’t verified around evaluation.
- Documentation and intent contracts still contradict the new behavior.

I’ll inspect the live goal, its worker evidence, and the state-sync worktree without changing workflow state. I’ll keep the failed historical goal separate from the one active live worker and won’t rebase, stash, merge, or discard anything.

The active goal has a live workflow worker and a dedicated worktree; it is only seconds into implementation, so there is no stall or loss to act on. I’m separately checking the state-sync failure because it is currently actionable, but I’ll only identify the dirty files and ownership boundary—not modify them.

The workflow claim is confirmed `running`, the provider process is alive, and the worker is actively inspecting the sync implementation and its Git test matrix. There is still no candidate commit or worktree change, which is normal at this early stage; no intervention is warranted yet.

The worker has now made a scoped implementation change: transient `*.lock`, `*.tmp`, and `.refine-sync-*` artifacts are excluded from durable state, stale transient artifacts on `refine/state` are removed, and failed copies clean their partial temp files. It also added focused tests and updated the Git workflow matrix. I’m leaving the worktree untouched while verification runs.

Focused verification passed after rustfmt corrected layout: the full `git_sync` unit-test module, including real temporary repositories/remotes, is green. The worker is now running `git diff --check`, formatting check, and clippy; the initial nonzero gate was a visible formatting-only failure and was corrected, not hidden.

The broader gate is now running. Before it started, all 14 state-sync tests, strict Clippy, rustfmt, and whitespace checks were green. No provider/authentication failure or worker loss is present; the existing state-sync dirty file remains the live supervisor chat record and has not been stashed or committed by me.

The full gate has progressed through Docker install/uninstall coverage and is now exercising the multi-instance state-sync integration test—the production-adjacent path most relevant to this fix. The worker and test processes remain healthy.

The repository-wide gate failed, and the failure is relevant: in `multi_instance_sync`, the second daemon did not observe the first daemon’s new Goal within 30 seconds. The worker explicitly surfaced it and is investigating fixture logs/state rather than treating it as flaky. No merge or workflow transition has occurred.

The multi-instance failure reproduced on an isolated rerun, so it is not being waived. The observable symptom is consistent: Goal creation returns 201 on daemon A, but the Goal file disappears before publication and daemon B remains empty; meanwhile `refine/state` repeatedly publishes only supervisor-agent updates. Investigation remains in scope and active.

The worker traced the integration failure to a Goal being published and then deleted by a later state-sync commit. It is now running the same multi-instance test against an isolated `origin/main` worktree to determine whether this is a pre-existing baseline failure or a regression from the candidate. That distinction will control scope; no candidate commit has been made yet.

The baseline comparison failed identically on untouched `origin/main`, confirming the multi-instance failure is pre-existing and caused by shared supervisor-state racing the fixture—not by this candidate. The worker kept scope narrow, reran the focused sync tests and formatting/whitespace checks successfully, then committed only the implementation and test-matrix files. I’m waiting for the workflow report and review transition.

Implementation is committed at `d1ae648b` and the isolated worktree is clean, but Refine has not yet settled the durable claim: the provider process exited while the Goal still says `in-progress` and the claim still says `running`. I’m allowing a short reconciliation window before treating that as lost-worker evidence; I will not force the transition.

Governance review failed the Goal with two concrete, retryable findings: cleanup happens after `git rebase`, so an interrupted sync can leave a tracked durable JSON edit that blocks recovery; and there is no test for restart from that dirty pre-rebase state. This is an actionable implementation failure, not a user-policy choice, so I’m checking the public retry operation. I will not modify the committed candidate or force any integration.

The public UI’s defined recovery path for a governance-failed Goal is “submit recovery round” and move `failed → todo`. That is a reversible, non-merge workflow action within the existing Goal, so I’m requeueing it with the two governance findings as the round prompt. No approval, merge, branch deletion, or worktree cleanup is involved.

Round 2 is live in a new isolated worktree. The worker correctly noticed the branch starts at `origin/main`, cherry-picked the prior candidate first, and is layering the recovery fix on top so the original work remains auditable.

The new restart regression is genuinely exercised and passes (`1 passed, 0 failed`): it proves no force-push/history rewrite, preserves the other node’s Goal, and commits the newer live mutation rather than the stale copied snapshot. The full state-sync unit group also passes. Two earlier invocations were visible command-selection/formatting corrections; neither was counted as verification.

Round 2 is committed as two auditable commits (`019aceca` preserved Round 1; `24269468` recovery). The worktree is clean, all 15 Git-sync tests pass, and strict lint/format/diff gates pass. A fresh post-implementation governance review is now running; I’m waiting for that verdict before reporting the Goal as reviewable.

The recovery round passed governance with no violations and reached `review`. The candidate branch was published; build orchestration and workflow QA completed, though QA is configured as disabled, so the meaningful evidence remains the 15 focused Git-sync tests plus lint/format/diff checks. Approval would integrate the candidate, so I’m stopping at the confirmation boundary and checking the final branch/health evidence for handoff.

The Goal is ready for user review; I did not approve or merge it.
